content.subtitle: Position of Adjectives
items: After verbs such as appear, be, become, feel, get, grow, look, seem, smell, sound, taste and turn
examples: She wore a **magnificent** gown to the ceremony.
By the end of the hike, my feet felt **sore**.

content.subtitle: Watch out!
items: The verbs in the table above are not normally followed by adverbs. However, some of them can be followed by adverbs when the verb refers to an action.
After as, how, so, this (= so), that (= so) and too, adjectives come before the article.
Some adjectives only appear after a verb and not before a noun. These include adjectives beginning with a-, such as afraid, aghast, alike, alive, alone, asleep, awake, etc.
examples: ✓ The security guard **looked suspiciously** at everyone who entered the building.
✓ I'd never want to work in as **demanding a role** as hers.
✓ It's **so/that demanding a role** that most people turn it down.
✓ How **rewarding a career** must medicine be!
✓ This is **too heavy a parcel** for me to carry on my own.
✓ A child was **asleep** in the back of the car.
✗ There was an **asleep** child in the back of the car.

content.subtitle: Order of Adjectives
content.description: When more than one adjective is used before a noun, they usually appear in the following order, sometimes separated by commas: judgement, size, shape, colour, origin, material, purpose
examples: They rented a **charming little stone cottage** by the sea.
She admired his **thick, dark, Italian, leather** jacket.
What the kitchen really needs is a **large pine dining table**.

content.subtitle: Comparative
content.description: to compare things or people that are different
examples: Their new apartment is much **larger** and **more modern** than ours.

content.subtitle: Superlative
content.description: to compare one member of a group of people or things with the whole group
examples: Cape Town is probably my **least favourite** city to drive in. I still think my home town is the **best place** I've ever lived.

content.subtitle: Comparative and Superlative Modifiers
items: Modifiers with comparatives: (quite) a bit, a great deal, a good deal, a little, (quite) a lot, any, considerably, even, far, just, little, much, no, slightly, somewhat
Modifiers with superlatives: by far, far and away, easily, far from, much, quite
examples: Since the new road opened, the town centre has become **considerably** more accessible and far less congested.
Most locals would agree that Riverdale is **easily** the most peaceful suburb to live in.

content.subtitle: Structures used to make comparisons
items: nothing like as ... as / nowhere near as ... as
examples: Silver is roughly **half** as expensive as gold.
Cotton isn't **nearly** as durable as nylon.
Cotton is **nothing like** as / nowhere near as durable as nylon.
The **longer** the delay, **the more frustrated** passengers become.
✓ Steel is not nearly as flexible as rubber. (large difference between the things being referred to)
✓ Today's session was not quite as productive as yesterday's. (small difference between the things being referred to)

content.subtitle: Uses
items: To refer to members of a general social group
To refer to members of a specific group
examples: Governments must do far more to support **the homeless**.
After the earthquake, **the displaced** were given emergency shelter.
**The Dutch** are internationally recognised for their innovative approach to water management.

content.subtitle: General Rules
content.description: There are three places in a clause where an adverb (or adverbial phrase) might appear: at the beginning, at the end and with the verb. Different kinds of adverb go in different positions, and some may go in more than one position.
examples: Adverbs do not normally appear between a verb and its direct object: ✗ She **explained very clearly** the problem. ✓ She explained the problem **very clearly**.
With verbs formed using auxiliary verbs, the adverb normally follows the first auxiliary: The bridge **has always attracted** photographers and tourists.
Adverbs of frequency (always, often, etc) follow auxiliary verbs and be and come before other verbs: He's **rarely** in his office on Fridays. He **rarely goes** to his office on Fridays.
Connecting adverbs usually go at the beginning of a clause: They invested all their savings in the property; **then**, the market collapsed.

content.subtitle: Definitions
items: **Ungradable** adjectives describe qualities which are extreme and which cannot be 'more' or 'less', eg amazing, dead, exhausted, fantastic, helpless, impossible, incredible, necessary, perfect, pointless, right, splendid, unacceptable, wonderful, wrong, etc.

content.subtitle: Modifiers
items: Modifiers with ungradable adjectives: absolutely, completely, quite, totally, utterly, etc
Modifiers with gradable adjectives: a bit, a little, fairly, quite, really, too, very, etc
examples: After running her third marathon of the year, Nina was **absolutely exhausted**.
Dan was **a bit tired** after the run, but he recovered quickly.

content.subtitle: Watch out!
content.description: When **quite** is used with gradable adjectives, it means 'rather, fairly'. When **quite** is used with ungradable adjectives, it means 'absolutely, completely'.
examples: ✓ The studio flat was **quite** nice, but a little cramped for two people.
✗ The view from the penthouse was **quite** magnificent! (= absolutely)

content.subtitle: Words with the same form as adjective and adverb
examples: ✓ The icy roads made driving **hard**.
✓ There was **hardly** any traffic on the motorway this morning.
✓ She trained **hard** every day in preparation for the competition.
✗ She trained too **hardly** and ended up injuring her knee.

content.subtitle: Watch out!
content.description: Some adjectives end in –ly, eg costly, deadly, friendly, likely, lively, lonely, lovely. They do not form adverbs, but we often use a phrase such as in a … way to describe how something is done.
examples: ✓ The staff always greet customers **in a very friendly way**.

sections.title: A: Adjective or Adverb?
sections.instructions: If the word in bold is correct, write OK. If it is incorrect, rewrite it correctly.

items.sentence: After the long flight, the passengers appeared **exhaustedly** in the arrivals hall.
By the end of the evening, it was clear that Marcus had grown **increasingly** suspicious of his new business partner.
This dish from the market stall tastes **oddly** – I think it may have gone off.
Nobody could understand why the new teacher seemed so **nervously** before the inspection.
She reached **carefully** into the bag, trying not to disturb anything inside.
The car turned **abruptly** down a narrow side street and disappeared from view.
The audience was growing **impatiently** as the curtain still hadn't risen.
Why are you staring at me so **intensely**?
Heat the sauce a little longer because it doesn't smell **spicily** enough yet.
Sophie doesn't get **offendedly** easily, but certain remarks genuinely upset her.
In the dark corridor, James felt **cautiously** along the wall until he found the light switch.
Victor looked **tiredly** after pulling a double shift at the hospital.

sections.title: B: Adjectives After as, how, so, too
sections.instructions: Complete the sentences. The words to use appear inside the hint pill.

items.sentence: I haven't had this ...... (night, late) in ages.
So ...... (neighbourhood, noisy) was it that we decided to move to a quieter part of the city.
If we'd known how ...... (complicated, repair) it was going to be, we'd have hired a professional.
It was as ...... (inspiring, lecture) as any I had attended in my entire career.
Sam has that ...... (natural, talent) he picks up new languages without any apparent effort.
It was too ...... (rare, opportunity) to pass up.
No matter how ...... (experienced, surgeon) you are, there is always something new to learn.
I don't think I'll ever be as ...... (skilled, negotiator) as my sister.

sections.title: C: Order of Adjectives
sections.instructions: Complete the sentences. The words to use appear inside the hint pill.

items.sentence: Oh, what a/an ...... (Chinese, exquisite, small) vase! Did you find it at the antique market?
Have you seen my ...... (brown, leather, waterproof) jacket? I was sure I left it on the back of the chair.
They stocked some ...... (cashmere, gorgeous, long, red, Spanish) shawls in the shop at the art gallery.
Didn't the gallery look stunning with those ...... (floor-length, golden, rich, velvet) curtains?
Does Harriet still keep that ...... (ginger, huge, Persian) cat of hers?
It's remarkable that such a ...... (Brazilian, slender, tiny) insect plays such a vital role in pollination.
Oliver has a ...... (antique, dark, elegant, walnut) desk in the corner of his home office.
The performer was wearing a ...... (dazzling, enormous, feathered) costume covered in sequins.
I still can't believe Maya turned up wearing that same ...... (enormous, furry, ridiculous) coat she had on last winter.
When are you going to get rid of those ...... (battered, green, old, velvet) armchairs in the study?

sections.title: D: Position of Adverbs
sections.instructions: Select the letter position(s) where the adverb in brackets should go. Some items have more than one correct answer.

items.sentence:
The Sydney Opera House, which (A) was (B) designed (C) by Danish architect Jørn Utzon, has become a globally recognised symbol of Australia.
Choose: **A / B / C / D**.

Construction began in 1959, and (A) the project (B) ran (C) over budget and behind schedule (D).
Choose: **A / B / C / D**.

Costing far more than the original estimate, (A) it (B) tested (C) the patience (D) of both the government and the public.
Choose: **A / B / C / D**.

Even Utzon, the architect originally responsible for the design, (A) admitted that it (B) would (C) have been (D) easier to manage costs had the project been handled differently.
Choose: **A / B / C / D**.

Despite the controversy, (A) visitors to the Opera House (B) had (C) a wonderfully positive impression (D).
Choose: **A / B / C / D**.

Even before its official opening, (A) it (B) was (C) regarded as the most ambitious structure in the southern hemisphere (D).
Choose: **A / B / C / D**.

sections.title: E: Comparative and Superlative Forms
sections.instructions: Complete with an appropriate comparative or superlative form of the words in the box. Add any other words you need.

items.sentence: I thought yesterday's seminar was far ……………………………………… this morning's, so things are definitely improving.
My new phone is ……………………………………………… my old one, though at least the camera is better.
Dr Patel knows exactly what she expects from her team, and I don't think I've ever worked for ……………………………………… supervisor.
Because of its unique molecular structure, graphene is ……………………………………… material ever discovered.
On the bank of the river, there stood ……………………………………… skyscraper I had ever laid eyes on.
If these negotiations drag on any ……………………………………… , both parties risk losing the deal entirely.
……………………………………………………………… his debut novel, the author's second book still sold over two million copies.
Even …………………………………………………………… amongst us can generally tell when someone has moved the furniture around.
I'm afraid what you're proposing wouldn't make ……………………………………… difference to the final outcome.
Frankly, I think ………………………………………………………… people who find out about this, the better.

sections.title: F: Comparative/Superlative Modifiers
sections.instructions: Circle the correct word or phrase.

items.sentence: It's encouraging to see that Nathan has become a great **lot / deal** more self-reliant since moving abroad.
The resort was **away / far** from the most exclusive I have ever stayed at.
The revised proposal is **less / little** more than a rewrite of the original draft.
This is **by / from** far the most disorganised conference I've ever attended.
Have you noticed that the children next door have grown **easily / considerably** taller since last summer?
My grandfather is getting **a little / little** forgetful these days, so we keep a close eye on him.
It was **quite / somewhat** the most breathtaking sunset I had ever witnessed!
If you have **any / even** further concerns about the timeline, please speak to the project manager directly.
The suspect the police described was **any / no** taller than the victim.
Valentina was told that she would need to prepare a **better / good** deal more thoroughly for the final round.

sections.title: G: Comparative Structures
sections.instructions: Complete the second sentence so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence, using the word given. Do not change the word given. You must use between three and eight words, including the word given.

items.sentence:
The blue sofa is slightly more expensive than the grey one. **quite**
The grey sofa ………………… the blue one.
items.answer: is not quite as expensive as

items.sentence:
Elena is far more dedicated to the project than Lucas seems to be. **like**
Lucas seems to be ………………… to the project.
items.answer: nothing like as dedicated as Elena

items.sentence:
If you sleep less, you will feel worse in the morning. **the**
You'll feel worse in the morning ………………… .
items.answer: the less you sleep, the worse

items.sentence:
My new bicycle was twice the price of my sister's. **expensive**
My sister's bicycle was ………………… mine.

Canada is far larger than France. **nowhere**
France ………………… Canada.
items.answer: is nowhere near as big as

items.sentence:
Tom is quite a bit more punctual than his colleague, Sandra. **nearly**
Sandra ………………… her colleague, Tom.
items.answer: is not nearly as punctual as

items.sentence:
Bigger cities tend to have higher levels of pollution. **the**
The ………………… the pollution.

Their opening night was considerably more successful than their closing night. **near**
Their closing night ………………… their opening night.
items.answer: was nowhere near as successful as

sections.title: H: Gradable/Ungradable Adjectives
sections.instructions: Underline the word or phrase which cannot complete the sentence.

items.sentence: We didn't all enjoy the show, but I personally thought it was **absolutely / quite / very** perfect.
You were **completely / fairly / totally** right about Marcus – he does live in Edinburgh.
We've been **absolutely / a little / quite** stressed at work, so a few days off would be wonderful.
It's **completely / totally / very** pointless trying to get Tom to change his mind once he's decided.
Clara seems to be **a little / really / utterly** anxious about the upcoming exam results.
Luis's goal from outside the penalty area was **absolutely / quite / too** incredible.
If you think I'm just going to forget what happened, you're **a bit / completely / quite** wrong.
In the end, the data the research team provided turned out to be **completely / totally / very** useless.

sections.title: I: Confusing Cases (hard/fairly/free/freely/high/highly)
sections.instructions: Complete using the words in the boxes. You need to use one word in each box twice.

items.sentence: Don't grip the handle so ……………………. or you'll wear it out before long.
This is …………………………… the most relaxing spot in the whole city, is it?
It's …………………………… to understand why the committee reached that particular decision.
It's not ……………………………! You always get to choose first!
Please share the workload …………………… among all the members of the team.
I'm …………………………… confident that the project is heading in the right direction.
The museum is offering ……………………… entry on the first Sunday of every month.
Blood circulates ……………………… throughout the body when you take regular exercise.
Children under twelve can get in ……………………… if they are accompanied by an adult.
She gets very dizzy in …………………………… buildings.
That sort of language is …………………………………… inappropriate for a professional environment.
The kite soared ………………………………… above the treetops.

sections.title: J: Cloze Text
sections.instructions: Write one word in each gap.

items.sentence: In recent decades, city governments have invested (1) ...... more heavily in cycling infrastructure than ever before, arguing that existing road designs were (2) ...... longer appropriate for modern urban life.
Every newly built cycle path placed a great (3) ...... more strain on urban planners trying to balance the competing needs of cyclists and drivers.
Dedicated lanes, designed to carry cyclists safely through busy intersections (4) ...... shared routes, began transforming the appearance of cities worldwide.
The (5) ...... cyclists used these new routes, the faster the case for further expansion grew, according to transport authorities.
At first, these dedicated cycle lanes proved (6) ...... popular with commuters than the older, unprotected routes they were designed to replace.
They were (7) ...... and away the (8) ...... cost-effective solution for short urban journeys, and they also had measurable health and environmental benefits.
Over time, however, cycle lanes became (9) ...... attractive to drivers, who resented the loss of road space and parking.
It also became clear that a dedicated cycle path was (10) ...... like as effective a long-term solution to congestion (11) ...... a fully integrated public transport network.
Cycling to work was more often (12) ...... not restricted to fair-weather months, limiting its appeal as a year-round alternative.
Despite the ongoing controversies, urban cycling continues to grow in popularity, and most experts now agree that well-designed infrastructure is (13) ...... element of any sustainable city.
I …………………………… when the phone rang at two in the morning.
Have you seen that ……………………… scarf she has been wearing all week?
Although she …………………………… to Shakespeare, many critics believe her writing is entirely original.
Don't you think ……………………… in a community have a responsibility to protect their local environment?
House prices in this district are ……………………………… the highest in the entire country.
The paramedics reached the scene and found ……………………………………………… .
To be fair, Rebecca has ………………………………… than you give her credit for.
I'm ………………………………… my older brother is.
options: B nothing near as ambitious as
D nowhere near as ambitious as
